title: Fettuccine Alfredo
subtitle: Roman pasta with butter and Parmesan — the original three-ingredient sauce
description: Fettuccine Alfredo as Romans make it contains exactly three ingredients: fresh fettuccine, cold butter, and aged Parmesan. No cream. The sauce forms through the vigorous tossing of the pasta in butter and Parmesan with a splash of pasta water — the starch emulsifies the fat into a glossy, coating sauce. The technique is the same as the mantecatura used to finish risotto. The result is richer and more intensely cheesy than any cream-based version.
sourceNotes: Created by Alfredo di Lelio at his Rome restaurant around 1908. The original recipe uses only fresh fettuccine, butter, and Parmesan — no cream. Recorded in early twentieth century Roman culinary accounts; the cream version is an American adaptation. The technique is described in Ada Boni's Il Talismano della Felicità (1927).

recipeTools.notes: Warmed — for tossing the pasta. A warm bowl prevents the butter from seizing.

glossaryTerms.definition: The starchy, salted water pasta cooks in. In this dish, a few tablespoons emulsify the butter and Parmesan into a silky coating sauce.

ingredients.notes: Or 200g dried — fresh cooks in 2–3 minutes
Cold, cut into cubes — the cold butter is what creates the emulsion
Finely grated on the smallest holes of a box grater — coarsely grated will not melt smoothly

steps: Warm a large mixing bowl by filling it with boiling water. Leave for 1 minute, then tip out and dry.
Bring a large pot of well-salted water to the boil. Cook the fettuccine until just done — 2–3 minutes for fresh, according to the packet for dried.
Reserve 100 ml of pasta water before draining.

Transfer the drained fettuccine to the warm bowl. Add the cold butter cubes and about 4 tbsp of the reserved pasta water. Toss vigorously with tongs for 1 minute — the butter will melt and begin to form a sauce.
Add two-thirds of the Parmesan and toss again, adding more pasta water a tablespoon at a time if the sauce seems too thick. Toss continuously until the sauce is glossy and coats each strand of pasta.
Divide between warm plates. Top with the remaining Parmesan and the coarsely ground black pepper. Serve immediately.

content.text: Two things determine whether this works: the Parmesan must be very finely grated (small-hole grater, not the large holes used for pizza), and the bowl must be warm before the pasta goes in. Cold butter dropped into a cold bowl seizes rather than melts into an emulsion. The
controls the consistency — add it sparingly.
Warm the bowl and cook the pasta

Fill a large mixing bowl with boiling water. Leave for 1 minute, tip out, and dry. Cook {{fresh-fettuccine}} of fettuccine in generously salted boiling water — 2–3 minutes for fresh. Reserve 100 ml of pasta water, then drain.

Transfer the fettuccine to the warm bowl. Add {{butter-unsalted}} of cold butter in cubes and 4 tbsp of the pasta water. Toss vigorously with tongs for 1 minute until the butter melts into a sauce. Add two-thirds of {{parmesan}} of Parmesan and toss again, adding more pasta water a tablespoon at a time if needed. The sauce should be glossy and cling to each strand. Divide between warm plates, top with the remaining Parmesan and coarsely ground black pepper.

The original Roman dish contains no cream. Adding cream produces a richer, heavier result but masks the quality of the Parmesan, which is the point of the dish. The no-cream technique also demonstrates the emulsifying power of pasta water — once understood, it applies to almost every Italian pasta sauce.
